metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit work item to update the `reducer/_processors.py` remedy-row body in `docs/architecture.md` (line 277) to replace the `_processors_keys.py` seam description with the new `_processors_identity.py` / 6-function seam, so the AC's `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` command actually passes.
evidence: docs/architecture.md line 277 contains: '...into a sibling `_processors_keys.py` once it clears the 100-LOC floor (today that seam alone is sub-floor)'
The plan asserts 'the stale sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is DELETED by this ticket' — but the actual occurrence is embedded in the _processors.py remedy-row body text, not a standalone row
grep result: `docs/architecture.md:277: | ... _processors_keys.py ...` — the AC's grep will match and the `! grep -q` assertion will therefore FAIL unless the embedded prose is also updated
findings.finding: The AC verification command `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` will fail on the unmodified file: the string `_processors_keys` appears in the body of the existing `reducer/_processors.py` remedy row at docs/architecture.md line 277 ('extract the identity-keyring processor cluster ... into a sibling `_processors_keys.py`'). The plan must also update or replace that prose, not merely delete a non-existent standalone table row.
findings.location: AC: 'The stale offenders-table row is deleted from docs/architecture.md. Verified by: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md`'
scenarios: Implementer deletes only a hypothetical standalone '_processors_keys.py' table row, leaves line 277 intact, and the AC command fails in CI

The architecture.md remedy row for _processors.py currently describes the old seam (_processors_keys.py, 4 functions); after this ticket lands it must be updated to describe the new seam (_processors_identity.py, 6 functions) — the plan's AC check is effectively testing that this prose update happened
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's work description, add: 'Update the docs/architecture.md offenders-table remedy row for reducer/_processors.py to replace the old `_processors_keys.py` / 4-function seam description with the new `_processors_identity.py` / 6-function seam description.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the `_fold_author_attribution` placement rationale: it is called by `process_create` (line 150), `process_comment` (line 394), and `process_revert` (line 520) which all remain in `_processors.py`, so moving it to the leaf forces a back-import from `_processors.py` — either keep it in `_processors.py` and import it into the leaf, or expand the move set to include all its callers, and update the 'true leaf' / 'back-edge cycle' rationale accordingly.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 150: `_fold_author_attribution(state, event)` inside process_create
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 394: `_fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)` inside process_comment
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 520: `_fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)` inside process_revert
Plan states cluster 'reference only each other (a true leaf)' — refuted because _fold_author_attribution has three callers outside the move set that remain in _processors.py
Moving _fold_author_attribution OUT while its callers remain IN requires _processors.py to import it back, creating the very back-edge the plan claims to prevent
findings.finding: The plan's back-edge-cycle justification for moving _fold_author_attribution is factually wrong: _fold_author_attribution is called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — all of which stay in _processors.py — not only by process_signature. Moving _fold_author_attribution to _processors_identity.py forces _processors.py to import it back from the leaf, which (a) breaks the 'true leaf / stdlib-free' claim, and (b) means the plan's 'else a back-edge cycle forms' rationale is inverted.
findings.location: Plan body: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms'
scenarios: Implementation as described creates an import of _fold_author_attribution back into _processors.py from the leaf, breaking the 'stdlib-free leaf with no upward imports' invariant
If the implementer instead duplicates _fold_author_attribution in both files, the two copies will diverge silently
If the implementer moves _fold_author_attribution only to the leaf without re-exporting, process_create/process_comment/process_revert break at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `_fold_author_attribution` in `_processors.py` and have `_processors_identity.py` import it from there (making the leaf not fully self-contained but avoiding cycles), OR expand the plan to acknowledge the import and retract the 'true leaf' claim for this function.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` greps the unanchored word '_processors_keys' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review__processors_keys'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"_processors_keys":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The plan declares the new leaf 'stdlib-free' as a design constraint but no AC verifies this property; an implementer who inadvertently imports a stdlib module (e.g., hashlib, dataclasses) in _processors_identity.py would pass all ACs.
findings.location: Plan body, first paragraph: 'NEW pure, stdlib-free leaf src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py'
findings.suggested_fix: If 'stdlib-free' means 'no rebar-internal back-edges' (i.e., only rebar imports are to siblings already imported by the leaf cluster), clarify the wording. If it literally means no stdlib, add: 'python -c "import ast, sys; tree=ast.parse(open(\"src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py\").read()); [sys.exit(1) for n in ast.walk(tree) if isinstance(n, ast.Import) and any(a.name in sys.stdlib_module_names for a in n.names)]"' or an equivalent ruff/import-linter check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the allowlist grep in AC4 to use a word-boundary-anchored or exact-match pattern (e.g., `grep -qE '(^|/)_processors\.py($| )'` or `grep -qF 'reducer/_processors.py'` with a full-path anchor) so it cannot match `_processors_identity.py`.
evidence: The pattern `'_processors.py'` is a literal substring of `'_processors_identity.py'`, so `grep -q '_processors.py' allowlist` returns 0 (match found) even when only `_processors_identity.py` appears in the file, causing the `!`-negated AC to report failure incorrectly.
The ceilings check correctly uses a trailing space anchor (`'reducer/_processors.py '`) to avoid this; the allowlist check does not.
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #1: substring false-positive — anchor the pattern with a word-boundary / quoted key.
findings.finding: The allowlist grep pattern `'_processors.py'` in AC4 is an unanchored substring match that will also match any line containing `_processors_identity.py` (e.g., if the new leaf is accidentally added to the allowlist), producing a false-positive failure or masking a genuine violation — a classic VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT #1 (substring false-positive).
findings.location: AC: `_processors.py removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` — proving command: `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`
scenarios: Executor correctly removes `reducer/_processors.py` from the allowlist but the grep still matches `reducer/_processors_identity.py` if present, causing a spurious CI failure and confusion about whether the AC is met.
Conversely, if `reducer/_processors.py` is only partially removed (e.g., a typo leaves a variant), the unanchored grep masks it.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` with `! grep -qE 'reducer/_processors\.py(\s|$)' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` — mirroring the trailing-space anchor already used in the ceilings check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4's allowlist grep to match the exact entry (e.g. use `grep -qF 'reducer/_processors.py'` or `grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py$'`) so it cannot false-match `_processors_identity.py`.
evidence: "! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt"
The new file is named _processors_identity.py, whose path contains the substring _processors.py
findings.finding: AC4's allowlist verify grep (`! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt`) matches any filename containing the substring `_processors.py`, including `_processors_identity.py`. If the new leaf were accidentally added to the allowlist, this check would incorrectly report failure, masking the real intent (verify that the OLD `_processors.py` entry is gone). The verify step should be precise about which entry it is checking for.
scenarios: If _processors_identity.py is added to the allowlist (e.g. as a precaution during migration), AC4's grep fires a false negative, failing the check even though the actual _processors.py entry was correctly removed.
Conversely, if _processors.py is removed but _processors_identity.py is accidentally added, AC4 passes when it should have flagged an issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` with `! grep -qF 'reducer/_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (or an anchored regex that matches the exact module path, not any path containing the substring).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's grep pattern to anchor on the exact path, e.g. `! grep -qF 'reducer/_processors.py ' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (note trailing space or use `grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py$'`) to avoid matching _processors_identity.py.
evidence: The string '_processors.py' is a substring of '_processors_identity.py'
.github/module-size-allowlist.txt uses bare paths like 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py'
grep -q '_processors.py' would match any line containing that substring, including 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py'
findings.finding: AC4's allowlist grep pattern `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` is an unanchored substring match that will ALSO match any line containing `_processors_identity.py`. If the new leaf is accidentally added to the allowlist (e.g. because it is over 800 LOC at first draft), the AC4 verification command will incorrectly report failure even when the original _processors.py is correctly removed.
scenarios: Executor verifies AC4 after a clean move but the new leaf accidentally ends up on the allowlist — the command exits 1 (falsely failing), causing confusion.

Alternatively the executor checks and finds '_processors_identity.py' on the allowlist, mistakenly concludes AC4 passes (since '_processors.py' pattern matched), while the OLD entry was never removed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` with `! grep -qF 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (the full path is unique and won't match the identity leaf's path).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the cycle-avoidance rationale to state the full constraint: _fold_author_attribution must live in the leaf (not in _processors.py) because process_signature — which moves to the leaf — calls it, making a leaf→_processors.py import the cycle-creating direction; the three remaining callers (process_create, process_comment, process_revert) in _processors.py consume it via the re-export, which is safe.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event) — called from process_create (staying in _processors.py)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event) — called from process_comment (staying in _processors.py)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event) — called from process_revert (staying in _processors.py)
The plan only states 'process_signature calls … _fold_author_attribution' as the sole cycle rationale, omitting the three other in-module callers that stay in _processors.py and rely on the re-export
findings.finding: The plan's cycle-avoidance rationale for moving _fold_author_attribution is incomplete: it names only the process_signature → _fold_author_attribution call edge, but _fold_author_attribution is also called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — all of which remain in _processors.py. The real cycle risk is that process_signature lives in the leaf and would import _fold_author_attribution from _processors.py, while _processors.py imports from the leaf — creating a genuine cycle. An implementer reading only the stated rationale might conclude the function can stay in _processors.py as long as process_signature is co-located, missing the actual import-direction constraint.
findings.location: Plan body — 'DEVIATION from docs/architecture.md' section: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.'
scenarios: An implementer reasoning from the stated rationale might attempt to keep _fold_author_attribution in _processors.py and only export it into the leaf (reversing the import direction), which would create the exact cycle the plan warns against.
A future plan reviewer auditing the cycle-avoidance claim finds the rationale incomplete and cannot verify it without reading the source.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.' with: '_fold_author_attribution MUST live in the leaf alongside process_signature (its co-leaf caller). If it stayed in _processors.py, the leaf would need to import it from _processors.py while _processors.py imports from the leaf — a genuine circular import. The three other callers of _fold_author_attribution (process_create, process_comment, process_revert) stay in _processors.py and reach it via the re-export, which is the safe direction.'

findings.finding: AC1 and AC2 proving commands (`wc -l …`) output a raw number but do not machine-enforce the stated bounds ([100,800] and ≤800 respectively); if the leaf is accidentally under 100 LOC or the residual file stays over 800, the command still exits 0 and the AC silently passes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (`wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py`) and AC2 (`wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`)
findings.suggested_fix:
AC1: `lines=$(wc -l < src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py) && [ "$lines" -ge 100 ] && [ "$lines" -le 800 ] && echo "ok ($lines lines)"`
AC2: `lines=$(wc -l < src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py) && [ "$lines" -le 800 ] && echo "ok ($lines lines)"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note or updated row to docs/architecture.md (or a linked ADR/comment) recording that `_processors_identity.py` supersedes the originally planned `_processors_keys.py` seam, why the scope was widened to 6 functions, and that it is compliant (within 100–800 LOC floor/cap).
evidence: Plan body: 'The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket' — no corresponding ADD or note is specified.
AC #5 only verifies deletion: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` — no AC verifies a new entry or rationale is present.
ABSENCE: no mention of adding an architecture.md entry for `_processors_identity.py` or annotating the deviation from the originally documented seam design.
findings.finding: The plan deletes the stale `_processors_keys.py` row from docs/architecture.md but adds no replacement entry or inline note capturing the deviation rationale (why the seam became `_processors_identity.py` with 6 functions instead of 4, and why the name changed). The decision survives only in the ticket body, leaving architecture.md silent on the refactor.
findings.location: docs/architecture.md update / AC item 5 ('The stale offenders-table row is deleted from docs/architecture.md')
scenarios: A future contributor reads architecture.md to understand the module-size split strategy and finds no record of the identity cluster or why the seam deviated from the originally planned 4-function keys split.
A reviewer auditing the offenders table sees _processors_keys.py gone with no explanation, and must search git history to reconstruct the rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/architecture.md, after deleting the stale `_processors_keys.py` offenders row, add a one-line comment or table row such as: `| reducer/_processors_identity.py | identity & attestation fold cluster (6 fns); supersedes planned _processors_keys.py seam; compliant ~130 LOC |` — or, if the table only tracks over-cap offenders, add a prose sentence in the relevant section noting the completed split and the rationale for the widened seam.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan body that _processors.py must import the moved helpers (_fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, attestation_kind) from the new leaf for its own internal callers (process_create, process_comment, process_revert, process_snapshot), not just re-export them for external consumers.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event)  [inside process_create, which stays]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)  [inside process_comment, which stays]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)  [inside process_revert, which stays]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:852: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...)  [inside process_snapshot, which stays]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:834: kind = attestation_kind(sig.get('manifest'), {})  [inside process_snapshot, which stays]
ABSENCE: plan names only _replay.py and external files as consumers needing the import; process_snapshot, process_create, process_comment, process_revert are not mentioned as needing the leaf import
findings.finding: The plan asserts the 6 moved symbols form 'a true leaf: reference only each other', but _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are also called by processors that STAY in _processors.py (process_create calls _fold_author_attribution at line 150 and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring at line 213; process_comment calls _fold_author_attribution at line 394; process_revert calls _fold_author_attribution at line 520; process_snapshot calls _bootstrap_genesis_keyring at line 852 and attestation_kind at line 834). This means _processors.py itself becomes a consumer of the leaf — the re-export mechanism handles external callers, but _processors.py's OWN remaining processors must also resolve these symbols, which requires importing them from the leaf. An implementor following the 'true leaf' description may not realize _processors.py needs a from ._processors_identity import ... line for its internal callers.
findings.location: Plan body: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)'
scenarios: An implementor moves the 6 symbols, adds re-exports for external callers, but forgets to update the call sites inside process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot — causing NameError at runtime since those symbols no longer resolve locally.

Even with the re-export, if the implementor adds 'from ._processors_identity import ...' only for external re-export and not as a usable local name, process_snapshot's direct calls to attestation_kind and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring break.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' with 'These are self-contained relative to external modules; _processors.py will import them back from the leaf both for re-export (# noqa: F401) and for its own remaining processors (process_create, process_comment, process_revert, process_snapshot) that call _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind.'

findings.finding: The plan claims the 6-symbol cluster 'reference only each other (a true leaf)' and that '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.' Both assertions are factually incomplete: _fold_author_attribution is also called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — all of which remain in _processors.py after the extract. Additionally, attestation_kind is called by process_snapshot (line 834) and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring by process_snapshot (line 852) and process_create (line 213), none of which are in the extract set. The cycle-prevention rationale therefore rests on an incomplete picture of the cross-cluster call graph. While the re-export mechanism still works (callers in _processors.py will use _fold_author_attribution via the module-level re-import), the implementor needs to know that _processors.py will have ACTIVE in-module call sites for 3 of the 6 extracted symbols — not just re-export stubs — and must arrange the imports accordingly without creating a circular import.
findings.location: Plan body — 'These reference only each other (a true leaf): process_key_event calls _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution. _fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' with: 'Within the leaf the call graph is self-contained (process_key_event→_rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature→attestation_kind+_fold_author_attribution). However _fold_author_attribution, attestation_kind, and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring are ALSO called by processors staying in _processors.py (process_create at line 150+213, process_comment at line 394, process_revert at line 520, process_snapshot at lines 834+852). _processors.py must therefore import these 3 symbols from _processors_identity at the top of the module (for use by the remaining callers) and then re-export all 6 with # noqa: F401 — the re-import lines serve both purposes.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
